truth.papers.A26.title: Making low alloy ultrahigh strength steel tough by high-density NiAl clusters
truth.papers.A26.paper_scope: A novel low alloy ultrahigh strength steel was developed by introducing extremely high-density NiAl clusters to achieve high toughness at a tensile strength of ~1.9 GPa.

prediction.papers.A26.title: Making low alloy ultrahigh strength steel tough by high-density NiAl clusters
prediction.papers.A26.paper_scope: Development of a novel low alloy ultrahigh strength steel containing extremely high-density coherent NiAl clusters and ε-carbides via dual-precipitation, achieving ~1.9 GPa tensile strength with enhanced toughness, and systematic investigation of precipitation behavior, dislocation regulation, and multi-scale toughening mechanisms.

prediction.alloys.alloy_1_3al.alloys_notes: Ni and Al concentrations were increased relative to the 0Al steel to ensure sufficient NiAl precipitation.

truth.interfaces.sample_1_3al_2h.defect_intera: NiAl clusters are sheared, promoting uniform dislocation distribution, suppressing pile-ups and local stress concentrations.
ε-carbides promote dislocation cell formation through Orowan bypassing.
truth.interfaces.sample_1_3al_2h.interface_not: Uniform KAM distribution (~1.31°) near fracture surface in 1.3Al steel indicates homogeneous strain, contrasting with inhomogeneous strain in 0Al steel.
NiAl clusters enhance uniform plasticity and delay crack initiation; secondary cracks and void coalescence deflect the main crack.
truth.interfaces.sample_1_3al_2h.phase_evoluti: NiAl clusters and ε-carbides nucleate independently during tempering at 180°C; Ni and Al do not compete with C partitioning. A fraction of NiAl clusters nucleates on Si-enriched banded zones, providing additional heterogeneous nucleation sites.

prediction.interfaces.sample_1_3al_2h.defect_intera: NiAl clusters promote uniform dislocation distribution, suppress pile-ups at boundaries, delay crack initiation, and increase lattice friction by reducing dislocation mean free path
prediction.interfaces.sample_1_3al_2h.interface_not: Uniform KAM distribution in 1.3Al steel (average 1.31° near fracture surface, 0.79° in matrix) versus inhomogeneous distribution in 0Al steel (average 1.16° with localized red/yellow high-strain regions)
prediction.interfaces.sample_1_3al_2h.phase_evoluti: During tempering at 180°C, ε-carbides and NiAl clusters nucleate and grow independently with no elemental overlap or competitive partitioning; a small fraction of NiAl clusters interacts with Si and precipitates along Si-Cr-Mo-enriched banded zones, providing heterogeneous nucleation sites.

truth.performance.sample_1_3al_2h.comparative_: 1.3Al,2h shows comparable tensile strength (~1.9 GPa) but significantly higher impact toughness (80 J vs 27.6 J for 300M)

prediction.performance.sample_1_3al_2h.comparative_: ~23 % higher impact absorption energy (KU2) and ~10.4 MPa·m^(1/2) higher fracture toughness (KIC) under comparable strength levels

truth.characterization_methods.sample_1_3al_2h: Chemical composition and spatial distribution of precipitates

ε-carbides: C ~8.91 at.%, no Ni/Al enrichment except slight at center; NiAl clusters: Ni+Al ~13 at.%, co-segregation of Si, Cr, Mo in banded zones; Si increases from 4.21 to 11.21 at.% in banded clusters.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_0al_2h.p: High-resolution imaging of martensite laths, retained austenite, and ε-carbides
truth.characterization_methods.sample_0al_2h.k: ε-carbides: 100–500 nm length, 3–10 nm width, aligned; retained austenite films at lath boundaries with K–S orientation relationship.

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_1_3al_2h: sampling regions near fracture surfaces for TEM analysis

NiAl cluster number density ~4.88 × 10^24 m^-3, equivalent radius ~0.57 nm; ε-carbide thickness ~2.2 nm, spacing ~10 nm; independent nucleation of ε-carbides and NiAl clusters without elemental overlap; slight Ni/Al enrichment in ε-carbide cores; Si-Cr-Mo co-segregation in banded NiAl clusters with Si rising to 11.21 at.%, Cr to 5.83 at.%, and Mo to 1.35 at.%
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_0al_2h.p: phase analysis and retained austenite quantification
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_0al_2h.k: Retained austenite content 1.0 % in 0Al steel and 1.8 % in 1.3Al steel; negligible diffraction contributions from carbides assumed

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.software_use: Jade 6.0; Aztec Crystal v2.1; CAMECA IVAS 3.6.8
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: XRD peak broadening validated against TEM microstructural observations for dislocation density estimation; KQ values calculated from three-point bending test data in accordance with ASTM E399
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: dislocation density and crystallite size estimation from XRD peak broadening
λ = 0.154 nm; D = apparent crystallite size; ε = lattice strain; b = Burgers vector magnitude
Equation: (Δ2θ cosθ)/λ = 0.9/D + (2ε sinθ)/λ; dislocation density ρ = 2√(3ε)/(Db). Applied to (110)M, (200)M, and (211)M diffraction peaks of the martensitic matrix.
conditional plane-strain fracture toughness from three-point bending
FQ = conditional load; S = loading span; a = crack length; B = thickness; W = width; f = dimensional factor depending on a/W
Three-point bending configuration with specimen thickness 25 mm, width 50 mm, and 3 mm fatigue pre-crack; loading rate 38 KN/min.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Figure 1(a) engineering stress-strain curves are referenced but exact curve tracings beyond tabulated mechanical properties are unavailable in the provided text pipeline.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Charpy U-notch impact absorption energy (KU2) values measured at room temperature: 0Al,WQ = 33 ± 6 J; 0Al,2h = 65 ± 3 J; 1.3Al,WQ = 23 ± 2 J; 1.3Al,2h = 80 ± 3 J.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Crack initiation energy (Ei) and crack propagation energy (Ep) derived from Charpy load-energy-displacement curves: 0Al steel Ei = 45.0 J and Ep = 17.4 J; 1.3Al steel Ei = 62.0 J and Ep = 21.0 J.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Charpy impact peak loads: 0Al = 39.7 kN, 1.3Al = 38.6 kN; deflections at peak load: 0Al = 2.06 mm, 1.3Al = 2.55 mm.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Shear fracture area on Charpy impact specimens: 0Al = 36.8 %, 1.3Al = 42.9 %.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Supplementary figures S1, S2, S3, and S5 are referenced in the text but their detailed content is not included in the provided text pipeline.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: The 1.3Al,WQ steel fractured prior to reaching its maximum tensile strength during tensile testing, though Table 2 reports an ultimate tensile strength of 2145 ± 144 MPa for this condition.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Multilevel martensitic structural dimensions for 0Al steel: packet size 3.4 ± 0.8 μm, block width 0.9 ± 0.2 μm, lath width 0.24 ± 0.07 μm; for 1.3Al steel: packet size 3.5 ± 0.9 μm, block width 0.8 ± 0.2 μm, lath width 0.23 ± 0.06 μm.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: APT proximity histogram data indicates carbon concentration up to ~8.91 at.% in ε-carbide cores and ~1.0 at.% carbon surrounding NiAl clusters, confirming independent nucleation without competitive partitioning between C and Ni/Al.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Toughening mechanisms in 1.3Al steel include large dimple growth and coalescence, uniform plastic deformation via dislocation homogenization, secondary crack formation, and extensive crack deflection due to void coalescence, which collectively increase crack tortuosity and energy absorption.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_10.value: During deformation, ε-carbides promote dislocation cell formation via bypassing, while closely spaced NiAl clusters are directly sheared, promoting a more uniform spatial dislocation distribution that suppresses localized strain accumulation.
